scope: Obstetrics & Gynaecology L1+L2 -> L3 or discard

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her GP. She had a Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 4 years and 11 months ago for contraception and heavy menstrual bleeding. Insertion was via a private clinic that printed a removal-reminder card stating 'replace at 5 years'. She has had no bleeding for 18 months, no pain, threads visible at the routine 4-yearly check. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, has no plans to conceive for at least 5 more years and would prefer to keep this device as long as possible. She specifically asks whether the device must be replaced this month or whether she can defer. Cycles, partner, BMI and screening history are all unremarkable.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding her current Mirena device?

new_options.text: Replace the device this month because the heavy-menstrual-bleeding indication caps duration at 5 years
new_options.rationale: The HMB indication is licensed for 5 years, but if her current need is contraception (HMB now resolved with amenorrhoea), the contraception licence governs duration. The device does not need replacement at 5 years for contraception (TGA PI Mirena 2023).

new_options.text: Replace the device this month because all LNG-IUS devices share a 5-year contraceptive licence in Australia
new_options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS was extended to 8 years for contraception by the TGA in 2023; Kyleena 19.5 mg remains 5 years. The licences are not shared (TGA PI Mirena; AMH 2024).

new_options.text: Continue the current device for contraception until 8 years from insertion, then replace
new_options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is now TGA-licensed in Australia for up to 8 years of contraception. Her HMB has resolved with amenorrhoea so the operative licence is contraception. She may safely defer replacement to the 8-year mark (TGA PI Mirena 2023; AMH 2024; Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Continue indefinitely as LNG-IUS devices are effective until menopause once inserted after age 25
new_options.rationale: There is no Australian licence for indefinite LNG-IUS use. A Mirena inserted at or after age 45 may be used until age 55 for endometrial protection on MHT, but that is a different scenario; this patient is 31 (Family Planning Australia; AMH).

new_options.text: Replace with a copper IUD now to take advantage of its 10-year licence
new_options.rationale: Switching to copper offers a longer interval but exposes her to heavier bleeding and removes the HMB benefit she may want preserved. There is no clinical indication to switch when the current LNG-IUS remains licensed for a further 3 years (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old woman attends a sexual-health clinic 96 hours after a single episode of condom failure. Her cycles are regular at 28 days; today is day 16 (intercourse occurred close to her predicted ovulation). She has a BMI of 33 and uses no regular medications. She declines an intrauterine device today because she is needle-phobic about the speculum/insertion. She has no allergies. She wants the oral option with the lowest failure rate given the 96-hour interval and her BMI, and she accepts that she will need to wait 5 days before resuming hormonal contraception if started on this method.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral emergency contraceptive in this scenario?

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: Levonorgestrel is licensed only up to 72 hours; she presents at 96 hours, so it is outside its window. Efficacy also declines with BMI above 26 and around ovulation (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel 3 mg orally as a single dose now, doubled for BMI
new_options.rationale: Although double-dose levonorgestrel is sometimes used off-label for higher BMI, it remains licensed only to 72 hours; at 96 hours it is outside the evidence-based window regardless of dose (Cochrane; Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: Ulipristal is licensed up to 120 hours, is more effective than levonorgestrel around ovulation, and has less BMI-related fall-off below 35. With the IUD declined, ulipristal is the best oral option at 96 hours (Family Planning Australia; Cochrane).

new_options.text: Yuzpe regimen using combined oral contraceptive pills now
new_options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is less effective than levonorgestrel or ulipristal and causes more nausea; it is now reserved for settings where dedicated emergency contraceptives are not available (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill today and abstain for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Starting the COCP today is for ongoing contraception, not emergency contraception; it provides no rescue effect for the unprotected intercourse 96 hours ago (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks presents with three weeks of progressive nausea and vomiting, six to ten episodes daily, unable to keep down most fluids, 3.5 kg weight loss (5%), PUQE score 14. She has tried small frequent meals, ginger and oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily without benefit. BP 102/68 with a 15 mmHg postural drop, HR 104, dry mucous membranes, ketones 3+ on urinalysis. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8. TSH normal. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy. She has been admitted, IV fluids with thiamine have been commenced and the team is choosing the next antiemetic step.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next antiemetic step?

new_options.text: Add oral doxylamine 12.5 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg four times daily as second-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Pyridoxine monotherapy has failed. RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend stepping up to doxylamine-pyridoxine combination as the next-line therapy before broader antiemetics, since safety data in pregnancy are best established (RANZCOG NVP/HG; eTG Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Switch directly to oral ondansetron 8 mg three times daily as monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for cases unresponsive to first- and second-line agents because of small first-trimester risks (orofacial clefts) and maternal QT effects; doxylamine-pyridoxine has not yet been trialled (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 16 mg three times daily today
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroids are last-line in refractory hyperemesis after failure of multiple antiemetics; using them now skips established earlier rungs of the ladder (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Discharge home on oral ginger and reassure that symptoms typically improve by 12 weeks
new_options.rationale: She has dehydration, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and >=5% weight loss, meeting criteria for hyperemesis admission; discharge without escalation is unsafe (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

new_options.text: Commence intramuscular prochlorperazine 12.5 mg every 6 hours as monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Phenothiazines may be used adjunctively but doxylamine-pyridoxine is preferred next-line per RANZCOG given the better pregnancy safety profile; IM prochlorperazine monotherapy is not the next step (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman is at 13 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain LMP confirmed on first-trimester ultrasound. She presents with a 48-hour history of fresh red vaginal bleeding which has now settled, and has passed several small clots at home. She is haemodynamically stable. At booking she was confirmed Rh(D) negative with a negative antibody screen, and the screen remains negative today. The previous pregnancy ended in a term vaginal birth without complications and she did not receive routine antenatal anti-D in that pregnancy (declined at the time). TVUS today shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy of 13 weeks 5 days with appropriate growth. The decision is to manage expectantly with follow-up in one week.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the appropriate anti-D immunoglobulin regimen for this presentation in Australian practice?

new_options.text: No anti-D is required as bleeding has settled and antibody screen is negative
new_options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage beyond 12 weeks in an RhD-negative non-sensitised woman is an indication for anti-D regardless of whether bleeding has settled (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: 250 IU is the dose for sensitising events before 12 weeks. From 13 weeks onwards, including threatened miscarriage, the recommended dose is 625 IU IM (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 625 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: From 13 weeks gestation onwards, sensitising events including bleeding in early pregnancy require 625 IU IM anti-D in non-sensitised RhD-negative women. Routine 1500 IU prophylaxis is given at 28 and 34 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Anti-D 1500 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: 1500 IU is the routine antenatal prophylaxis dose given at 28 and 34 weeks; it is not the dose for a sensitising event at 13 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Withhold anti-D until Kleihauer testing confirms feto-maternal haemorrhage
new_options.rationale: Kleihauer testing is not required for sensitising events before 20 weeks; anti-D must be given within 72 hours regardless. Kleihauer is reserved for events from 20 weeks onwards to guide additional dosing (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 woman attends her midwife at 35 weeks and 4 days. Her two prior births were uncomplicated term vaginal deliveries; her second neonate developed early-onset GBS sepsis confirmed on blood culture at 18 hours of life and recovered fully after IV benzylpenicillin. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated with normal antenatal serology and OGTT. There has been no preterm labour, no PROM, no fever and no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy. The local maternity service offers either universal culture-based screening at 35-37 weeks or a risk-factor pathway, and her midwife is planning intrapartum management.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding GBS screening and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for this woman?

new_options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab now and only give intrapartum prophylaxis if positive
new_options.rationale: A previous infant with invasive GBS disease is a stand-alone indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regardless of swab result; screening is not required for this woman (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

new_options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36-37 weeks and decide prophylaxis on the result
new_options.rationale: Even with a swab inside the recommended 35-37 week window, a previous infant with invasive GBS disease still mandates intrapartum prophylaxis irrespective of the result (RANZCOG; CDC).

new_options.text: Do not screen; give intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin in labour regardless of swab status
new_options.rationale: A previous neonate with invasive early-onset GBS disease is an automatic indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis; screening adds no value and could falsely reassure if negative (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

new_options.text: Do not screen and do not give intrapartum prophylaxis as she had no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy
new_options.rationale: GBS bacteriuria in the index pregnancy is one indication but is not the only one. A prior infant with invasive GBS is an independent indication that overrides absence of bacteriuria (RANZCOG; CDC).

new_options.text: Screen on admission in labour using a rapid intrapartum PCR and treat if positive
new_options.rationale: Intrapartum PCR is not the standard pathway in Australia, and again, a previous infant with invasive GBS mandates prophylaxis regardless of the test (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her GP using clinician-collected liquid-based cytology and HPV. She has had two previous CSTs, both negative for oncogenic HPV. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14. She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no pelvic pain. Today's result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is not pregnant.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 12 months
new_options.rationale: The 12-month repeat pathway applies to HPV non-16/18 with negative or pLSIL reflex cytology, not pHSIL or HSIL (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 5 years as her prior history is reassuring
new_options.rationale: A positive HPV result with high-grade cytology requires escalation not a return-to-routine interval, regardless of prior screening or vaccination history (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Refer directly for colposcopy
new_options.rationale: Under the NCSP, HPV non-16/18 with pHSIL or HSIL on reflex cytology mandates direct colposcopy referral, the same as any HPV 16/18 result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Refer directly for diagnostic cone biopsy without colposcopy
new_options.rationale: Cone biopsy is a treatment after colposcopic diagnosis, not a primary diagnostic step from a screening result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab in 12 months to confirm the result
new_options.rationale: Self-collection is for routine screening, not for follow-up of a high-grade result; colposcopy is required now (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her child-health-nurse appointment 6 weeks after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. She describes 4 weeks of low mood, poor sleep beyond infant needs, anhedonia and intrusive thoughts she is a 'bad mother'. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to the baby. She has a supportive partner and is breastfeeding well. There is no history of bipolar disorder, prior psychiatric admissions or postpartum psychosis. The nurse administers the COPE-recommended screening instrument: she scores 16 out of 30 with item 10 (self-harm thoughts) scored 0. There are no language or literacy barriers.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat the EPDS in 2-4 weeks without GP referral as the score is below threshold
new_options.rationale: An EPDS score of 13 or more in the postnatal period meets the threshold for further clinical assessment under COPE guidance; 16 with persistent symptoms is not below threshold (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Refer to her GP for clinical assessment for postnatal depression within 1-2 weeks
new_options.rationale: An EPDS score >=13 with item-10 zero (no acute self-harm risk) indicates probable depression requiring GP clinical assessment within a short window. Acute escalation is not needed; routine perinatal mental-health follow-up is not sufficient (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Refer to the emergency department for acute psychiatric assessment today
new_options.rationale: ED referral is reserved for active suicidality, infanticidal ideation, psychotic features or rapid decline; item-10 zero with no risk features does not warrant ED (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily today without GP review as it is safe in breastfeeding
new_options.rationale: Antidepressant initiation is a medical decision after diagnostic assessment and informed consent; the child-health nurse should not prescribe without GP review (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

new_options.text: Reassure that this represents normal 'baby blues' and review at the 8-week immunisation visit
new_options.rationale: Baby blues resolve by 2 weeks postpartum; symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks with EPDS 16 indicate probable postnatal depression, not blues (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 18 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 45-90 days), facial and chest hair growth requiring weekly waxing, weight gain of 6 kg over 12 months, and acanthosis nigricans. BMI 29, modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12. Total testosterone is at the upper limit of normal, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated, 17-hydroxyprogesterone normal, TSH and prolactin normal, beta-hCG negative. Pelvic ultrasound is technically suboptimal due to bowel gas: each ovary appears to contain 'multiple small follicles' but follicle count cannot be quantified and ovarian volumes are not reported. Anti-Mullerian hormone is 58 pmol/L (markedly elevated).
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step under the 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline?

new_options.text: Diagnose PCOS now on the basis of oligo-anovulation plus clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism
new_options.rationale: The 2023 guideline confirms that any two of (oligo-anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS or elevated AMH) meet criteria in adults; this patient meets the first two so the diagnosis is established without USS or AMH (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until a repeat pelvic ultrasound quantifies follicle count and ovarian volume
new_options.rationale: USS is not required once two other Rotterdam features are present, and the 2023 guideline accepts AMH as an alternative to ultrasound; further imaging adds no diagnostic value here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis as elevated AMH alone is sufficient and other criteria are not required
new_options.rationale: AMH or polycystic morphology in isolation is not diagnostic; two of three Rotterdam-derived features remain required in adults (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until LH:FSH ratio confirms elevated LH consistent with PCOS
new_options.rationale: LH:FSH ratio is not part of the Rotterdam-derived criteria and is not required for diagnosis (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

new_options.text: Defer diagnosis until adrenal hyperplasia is excluded with an ACTH stimulation test
new_options.rationale: 17-hydroxyprogesterone is already normal which adequately excludes non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia; ACTH stimulation is unnecessary here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, BP 122/76 mmHg, with regular 28-day cycles and a normal cervical screening 12 months ago. Over the past year she has had three episodes of unilateral throbbing headache lasting 24 hours, each preceded by a 20-minute episode of zigzag visual lines in one visual field followed by partial right-sided pins and needles. Headaches are now occurring monthly. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, ischaemic heart disease, breast cancer or liver disease. She is not breastfeeding. She has heard friends recommend the combined oral contraceptive pill and asks specifically about that option.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in contraceptive counselling?

new_options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 20 mcg ethinyloestradiol to minimise vascular risk
new_options.rationale: Migraine with aura at any dose of ethinyloestradiol is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable health risk) because of stroke risk. A lower dose does not change the category (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol and levonorgestrel
new_options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 in migraine with aura regardless of progestogen choice; the ischaemic stroke association is driven by oestrogen exposure (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Recommend a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method as combined hormonal contraception is contraindicated
new_options.rationale: Visual aura preceding headache is migraine with aura, UKMEC 4 for all combined hormonal contraception. Progestogen-only methods (POP, DMPA, implant, LNG-IUS) and copper IUD are UKMEC 1-2 and safe alternatives (UKMEC 2016).

new_options.text: Continue current condom-only contraception and only review if she develops new neurological deficit
new_options.rationale: Failure to offer a more reliable method is suboptimal; multiple effective progestogen-only and non-hormonal options remain available (Therapeutic Guidelines).

new_options.text: Refer to neurology before any contraceptive can be safely commenced
new_options.rationale: Migraine with aura is a clinical diagnosis from history; neurology referral is not required to begin a UKMEC 1-2 progestogen-only or non-hormonal contraceptive today (UKMEC 2016; RACGP).

verdicts.discard_reason: Pure recall of Rotterdam criteria for PCOS diagnosis; duplicates obgyn-016 which has been revised to a richer alternative-selection L3. Topic does not support a second distinct L3 question without contrivance.

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 7 months of secondary amenorrhoea, intermittent bilateral galactorrhoea and reduced libido. She has had no recent pregnancy or breastfeeding, no headaches and no visual symptoms. She started risperidone 4 mg daily 9 months ago for chronic schizoaffective disorder, prescribed by her psychiatrist, and is responding well with no recent admissions. She takes no other regular medications and uses no recreational drugs. BMI 24, BP 116/70. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Bloods: beta-hCG negative, repeat mid-morning serum prolactin 1900 mIU/L (reference <500) on two occasions one week apart, TSH and free T4 normal, U+Es normal. She is keen to conceive in the next 12 months.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Request urgent MRI pituitary with gadolinium before any further investigation
new_options.rationale: MRI is indicated for unexplained sustained hyperprolactinaemia, but when a dopamine-antagonist antipsychotic is the strongly suspected cause, identification and management of that cause should precede imaging (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Liaise with the treating psychiatrist about switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic such as aripiprazole, then re-check prolactin
new_options.rationale: Risperidone is a strong dopamine D2 antagonist and a common cause of pharmacological hyperprolactinaemia. The first step is collaborative medication review with the prescribing psychiatrist; switching to a prolactin-sparing agent (aripiprazole) and re-measuring before imaging avoids unnecessary MRI and identifies the cause (Endocrine Society 2011; eTG Endocrinology).

new_options.text: Commence cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly empirically and reassess prolactin in 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: Dopamine agonists may worsen psychosis in this population and are not first-line when a drug-induced cause is highly likely; medication review is the correct next step (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Macroprolactin screen with polyethylene glycol precipitation as the next test
new_options.rationale: Macroprolactin screening is for asymptomatic hyperprolactinaemia. This woman has symptoms (amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, low libido) consistent with biologically active hyperprolactinaemia; the focus should be on the offending drug (Endocrine Society 2011).

new_options.text: Stop risperidone abruptly without psychiatric consultation to allow prolactin to normalise
new_options.rationale: Abruptly stopping antipsychotics without psychiatric input risks relapse and is unsafe; switching requires shared decision-making (Endocrine Society 2011).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman is reviewed 8 days after IVF oocyte retrieval (21 oocytes). She reports 48 hours of worsening abdominal distension, nausea and reduced oral intake. BP 108/64, HR 108, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air. Abdomen distended, tense ascites palpable to the costal margin, mild generalised tenderness without peritonism. Pelvic ultrasound: ovaries 11 cm right and 10 cm left with multiple cysts; large-volume ascites extending above the umbilicus; small right pleural effusion. Bloods: haematocrit 0.48 (baseline 0.38), WCC 13.2, sodium 132, potassium 4.6, creatinine 96 micromol/L (baseline 62), albumin 27 g/L, ALT 78 U/L, urine output 25 mL/h for the past 4 hours.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which classification and management plan best fit her ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome?

new_options.text: Moderate OHSS: discharge home with daily phone review and oral fluids
new_options.rationale: Haematocrit >=0.45, rising creatinine, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia and ascites above the umbilicus place her into severe OHSS, not moderate; outpatient management is unsafe (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit for IV crystalloid, VTE prophylaxis with LMWH, strict fluid balance and consider paracentesis
new_options.rationale: Haematocrit 0.48, oliguria, creatinine rise, hypoalbuminaemia, ascites above the umbilicus and pleural effusion meet severe OHSS criteria. Management is admission for IV fluids, prophylactic LMWH (high VTE risk), strict input/output and paracentesis for symptomatic tense ascites (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Critical OHSS: transfer to ICU now for invasive ventilation and renal replacement
new_options.rationale: Critical OHSS requires anuria, ARDS, thromboembolism or multiorgan failure. She has oliguria not anuria, normal saturations and no thromboembolism; ICU is not yet required (RANZCOG OHSS).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but give large-volume normal saline boluses until haematocrit normalises
new_options.rationale: Aggressive saline boluses worsen third-spacing in OHSS; balanced fluid strategy with albumin where indicated, plus paracentesis, is preferred (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but withhold thromboprophylaxis until haemoconcentration resolves
new_options.rationale: OHSS is a hypercoagulable state and LMWH thromboprophylaxis is recommended early; withholding it increases life-threatening VTE risk (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old woman and her 40-year-old male partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. The woman has regular ovulatory cycles, mid-luteal progesterone confirms ovulation, AMH 6 pmol/L (low for age), antral follicle count 6, TSH and prolactin normal, BMI 24, HyCoSy confirmed bilateral tubal patency. The partner has had two normal semen analyses by WHO 2021 criteria 4 weeks apart. Neither has had pelvic surgery, endometriosis or STI. They are non-smokers and drink within recommended limits. They have not had any prior pregnancies. They ask which ART pathway gives the highest live-birth chance given her age and ovarian reserve.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Continue timed intercourse for a further 12 months before referral to a fertility specialist
new_options.rationale: Female age >=35 with infertility >=6-12 months warrants prompt referral; an additional 12 months reduces cumulative live-birth chance, particularly with low AMH (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Proceed to in vitro fertilisation given her age and low ovarian reserve
new_options.rationale: At 38 with AMH 6 pmol/L and AFC 6 (low ovarian reserve), IVF gives the highest cycle live-birth chance and allows aneuploidy management; IUI is less effective in this context (NICE CG156; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Three cycles of intrauterine insemination with clomiphene before considering IVF
new_options.rationale: IUI offers limited additional benefit over expectant management in unexplained infertility with low ovarian reserve at 38; cycles spent on IUI may reduce her IVF window (NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Commence clomiphene citrate alone with timed intercourse for six cycles
new_options.rationale: She is already ovulating; clomiphene without IVF or IUI does not address her age-related and reserve-related decline (NICE CG156).

new_options.text: Recommend donor oocyte IVF as her age and reserve preclude autologous IVF success
new_options.rationale: Autologous IVF still offers reasonable success at 38 with AMH 6; donor oocyte is reserved for those with very low reserve and repeated IVF failure (RANZCOG; NICE).

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman has 9 months of progressively heavy menstrual bleeding and intermenstrual spotting. Hb 102 g/L (microcytic) responsive to iron, TSH and beta-hCG normal, endometrial biopsy benign, cervical screening up to date. Pelvic ultrasound and saline-infusion sonography demonstrate a single 3 cm fibroid lying entirely within the endometrial cavity on a narrow stalk (FIGO type 0), with no other fibroids and a normal myometrium. She has completed her family, wishes to preserve her uterus, and prefers the most targeted intervention with the fastest return to normal activity. She has no contraindication to anaesthesia.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for her fibroid-related heavy menstrual bleeding?

new_options.text: Hysteroscopic resection of the submucosal fibroid
new_options.rationale: A FIGO type 0 submucosal fibroid causing HMB is best treated by hysteroscopic resection: targeted, uterus-preserving, day-procedure, with rapid recovery and high HMB resolution rates (RANZCOG fibroids; FIGO PALM-COEIN).

new_options.text: Uterine artery embolisation by interventional radiology
new_options.rationale: UAE is effective for symptomatic intramural or larger fibroids but is suboptimal for an isolated cavity-based pedunculated fibroid (FIGO 0), which may necrose into the cavity; hysteroscopic resection is preferred (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion at next routine appointment
new_options.rationale: LNG-IUS efficacy is reduced when a submucosal fibroid distorts the cavity, and expulsion rates are higher; hysteroscopic resection should precede or replace LNG-IUS in FIGO 0 lesions (RANZCOG; NICE).

new_options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive treatment
new_options.rationale: She wishes to preserve her uterus and the lesion is well suited to a uterus-sparing day procedure; hysterectomy is disproportionate (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Three-month course of GnRH agonist (goserelin) as definitive management
new_options.rationale: GnRH agonists are used pre-operatively to shrink fibroids and treat anaemia but are not definitive; bleeding returns once stopped, and adverse effects limit long-term use (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour, worse after intercourse. She has no vulval itch, no dysuria, no abnormal bleeding and no fever. She is in a monogamous relationship of 18 months using a copper IUD, last cervical screening 14 months ago was normal. Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall 5.2; KOH whiff test positive; wet mount shows clue cells, scant lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. She is not currently pregnant. She had a similar episode 5 months ago which was treated with a 7-day course of oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily; she completed the course, and symptoms resolved for 4 months before recurring.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Repeat the same 7-day oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily course
new_options.rationale: This is recurrent BV (>=3 episodes in 12 months or symptomatic recurrence after a complete treatment). Simply repeating the standard course alone will not address the high recurrence rate; suppressive maintenance therapy after re-induction is recommended (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Treat with oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days, then commence intravaginal metronidazole gel twice weekly for 4-6 months as suppressive therapy
new_options.rationale: Recurrent BV in Australia is managed with re-induction (oral or intravaginal metronidazole) followed by intravaginal metronidazole or clindamycin twice weekly as suppression for several months to reduce recurrence (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Reassure that this is normal discharge and no treatment is needed
new_options.rationale: Amsel criteria are met (homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells). Symptomatic BV warrants treatment; reassurance alone is inappropriate (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Empirically treat both her and her male partner with metronidazole
new_options.rationale: Routine treatment of male partners of women with recurrent BV is not currently standard in Australian guidelines, although the evidence base is evolving. Suppressive therapy for the index patient is the established next step (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

new_options.text: Remove the copper IUD as it is the underlying cause of recurrent BV
new_options.rationale: The copper IUD is not a clear cause of BV and removal is not first-line; suppressive therapy is preferred before considering device change (Family Planning Australia).

verdicts.new_stem: A 21-year-old woman at 18 weeks of pregnancy presents with multiple soft non-keratinised fleshy lesions on the vulva and perianal area, first noticed 6 weeks ago and increasing in number. She is otherwise well with an uncomplicated pregnancy on midwifery shared care. She did not receive the HPV vaccine in school. On examination there are approximately 10 papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm distributed over the labia majora and perianal area; no internal vaginal, cervical or intra-anal lesions; cervical screening normal 4 months ago. She is keen to treat them before delivery and prefers home application.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her anogenital warts?

new_options.text: Self-applied podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution twice daily for 3 days, then 4 days off, repeated up to 4 cycles
new_options.rationale: Podophyllotoxin is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity (Australasian STI Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Self-applied imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week for up to 16 weeks
new_options.rationale: Imiquimod is not recommended in pregnancy because of limited safety data; podophyllotoxin and imiquimod should both be avoided (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Clinician-applied cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen weekly
new_options.rationale: Cryotherapy is safe in pregnancy and is the preferred first-line treatment for anogenital warts in pregnant women in Australian guidelines; trichloroacetic acid is an alternative. Self-applied options are contraindicated (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Watch and wait until after delivery as warts always regress postpartum
new_options.rationale: Warts may proliferate during pregnancy and can grow large; treatment is offered antenatally to reduce burden at delivery, not deferred indefinitely (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

new_options.text: Plan elective caesarean section solely to prevent vertical HPV transmission
new_options.rationale: Caesarean is not indicated solely for HPV transmission risk; vaginal delivery is acceptable unless warts obstruct the birth canal (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-year-old woman is being treated for chlamydia trachomatis (NAAT positive on self-collected vaginal swab; gonorrhoea negative). She is on the etonogestrel implant, has no penicillin allergy, no pelvic or abdominal pain, no cervical motion tenderness, and a normal bimanual examination. She reports anal intercourse with two regular male partners in the past 3 months and asks for treatment that covers all sites where she could have acquired infection. She is not pregnant. She has heard from a friend that 'a single azithromycin pill' is the treatment.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antimicrobial regimen for this woman in Australian practice?

new_options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose
new_options.rationale: Australian and Therapeutic Guidelines now recommend doxycycline as first-line for genital chlamydia and have moved away from single-dose azithromycin, which is inferior for rectal infection (eTG; ASHM).

new_options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Doxycycline 100 mg bd for 7 days is first-line for uncomplicated chlamydia in non-pregnant adults, including rectal infection where doxycycline is markedly superior to azithromycin (eTG; Australasian STI Guidelines).

new_options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 21 days
new_options.rationale: 21 days is the regimen for lymphogranuloma venereum, not uncomplicated genitorectal chlamydia (Australasian STI Guidelines).

new_options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin is reserved for chlamydia in pregnancy where doxycycline is contraindicated; she is not pregnant (eTG).

new_options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is gonococcal therapy and would not cover chlamydia (eTG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman who is breastfeeding her 4-month-old infant (exclusive breastfeeding, no formula) presents with 3 weeks of frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulval irritation and dyspareunia. Strawberry cervix on speculum, vaginal pH 5.6, KOH whiff positive, saline wet mount shows motile flagellated protozoa. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia NAAT have been sent. She has had two new male partners in the past 4 months. She has no allergies and no liver disease. She wishes to continue breastfeeding without interruption if possible.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

new_options.text: Single 2 g oral metronidazole dose to her and her recent partners; pause breastfeeding for 12-24 hours and discard expressed milk
new_options.rationale: Single-dose metronidazole 2 g is first-line for trichomoniasis in adults, including breastfeeding women, with brief interruption of breastfeeding (12-24 hours) and discarding milk during peak levels to minimise infant exposure. Partner treatment is essential to prevent reinfection (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).

new_options.text: Intravaginal metronidazole 0.75% gel nightly for 5 nights; no partner treatment
new_options.rationale: Topical metronidazole has inadequate efficacy for trichomoniasis (cure rate <50%); systemic therapy is required and partners must be treated (Australasian STI Guidelines).

new_options.text: Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with continued breastfeeding throughout
new_options.rationale: Tinidazole is effective but has a longer half-life requiring a longer breastfeeding pause (about 3 days); metronidazole single dose is preferred here (Australasian STI Guidelines).

new_options.text: Permanently cease breastfeeding so that metronidazole can be given safely
new_options.rationale: Cessation of breastfeeding is not required; a brief 12-24-hour pause is sufficient (Australasian STI Guidelines).

new_options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg bd for 7 days to her only
new_options.rationale: Doxycycline does not cover trichomonas and is contraindicated in breastfeeding due to dental effects (eTG; ASHM).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+5 in spontaneous labour has been at full dilatation with a working epidural for 2 hours. She has been pushing actively for 90 minutes. The foetal head is at 0 station in occiput transverse with moderate caput and 1+ moulding. CTG: baseline 145, variability 5-10, no accelerations, occasional early decelerations. Contractions 3 in 10 lasting 45 seconds, palpate moderate. Liquor clear. EFW 3500 g, gynaecoid pelvis. She is exhausted but cooperative.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Continue active pushing for a further hour as delay is defined only after 3 hours in nulliparous women with epidural
new_options.rationale: Although 3 hours of pushing is the time threshold, descent has stalled at 0 station with malposition, caput and moulding. Continued pushing risks foetal compromise and obstructed labour; reassessment is needed now (RANZCOG IOL/labour).

new_options.text: Obstetric review for assessment of arrest of descent with consideration of rotational instrumental or caesarean
new_options.rationale: After 90 minutes of pushing with no descent below 0 station, malposition (OT), caput, moulding and inadequate contractions, an experienced obstetrician must assess for instrumental delivery (rotational) versus second-stage caesarean rather than continuing routine pushing (RANZCOG; NICE intrapartum).

new_options.text: Immediate Kielland's rotational forceps in the room without senior review
new_options.rationale: Rotational instrumental delivery is high-risk and requires an experienced operator, often in theatre with trial-of-instrumental protocols; bypassing senior review is unsafe (RANZCOG OVB).

new_options.text: Commence oxytocin augmentation and continue pushing for another 2 hours
new_options.rationale: Augmentation may be considered if there is descent and inadequate contractions, but with malposition at 0 station, caput and moulding, oxytocin without resolution of malposition risks uterine rupture and obstructed labour (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Stop pushing and observe passively for spontaneous descent for the next 4 hours
new_options.rationale: Passive observation is reasonable before active pushing begins but not after 90 minutes of active pushing with stalled descent and malposition; this prolongs risk of foetal acidosis (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 39-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 with a BMI of 34 attends antenatal clinic. EDD has been confirmed by an 8-week scan. The foetus is cephalic, growth on the 50th centile, normal AFI and Dopplers, reassuring CTG today. Bishop score 4 (cervix 1 cm, 30%, firm, posterior, station -2). Booking BP 118/74, today 122/76 with no proteinuria. She has gestational diabetes diet-controlled with normal home glucose monitoring. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 40 weeks with no complications. She is anxious about a long pregnancy but feels well. She asks about timing of induction.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which evidence-based threshold best supports a recommendation to induce labour for this woman?

new_options.text: Maternal age >=35 alone independently justifies induction at 39 weeks regardless of other factors
new_options.rationale: Maternal age >=39-40 with multiple risk factors is often used, but age alone is not a strong stand-alone trigger at 39+0. Stronger triggers in this woman are GDM and elevated BMI (RANZCOG; NICE).

new_options.text: Diet-controlled GDM with appropriate growth supports induction at 39-40+6 weeks to reduce stillbirth risk
new_options.rationale: Australian/RANZCOG guidance supports IOL between 39 and 40+6 weeks for diet-controlled GDM to reduce stillbirth and shoulder dystocia, particularly with BMI elevation and age. This is the strongest evidence-based reason at this gestation (RANZCOG; ADIPS).

new_options.text: BMI 34 alone is an evidence-based indication for induction before 39+0 weeks
new_options.rationale: Class I obesity without other risk factors does not by itself mandate induction before 39 weeks; combined with GDM it is supportive but not a stand-alone trigger (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Bishop score >=8 is required before induction can be offered at any gestation
new_options.rationale: An unfavourable cervix does not preclude IOL; cervical ripening (prostaglandins, Foley) precedes oxytocin. Bishop score is not a gatekeeper (RANZCOG IOL).

new_options.text: Induction at 39+0 reduces caesarean section rates compared with expectant management to 41+0
new_options.rationale: The ARRIVE trial suggested a small reduction in caesarean in low-risk nulliparas with 39-week induction but this is not the primary evidence base in this Australian high-risk multipara (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+2 is at full dilatation, head at +3 station, direct OA, distending the perineum. CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 12 minutes. The consultant has decided to perform a non-rotational Neville-Barnes forceps delivery in the room. Bladder empty, epidural topped up, consent obtained, paediatric team notified. EFW 3500 g, pelvis gynaecoid, head fully engaged. The registrar suggests cutting a midline episiotomy at the 12 o'clock position to maximise space.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which response is most appropriate regarding episiotomy in this birth?

new_options.text: Decline midline episiotomy; cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 60 degrees from the midline at crowning
new_options.rationale: Mediolateral episiotomy at approximately 60 degrees from the midline at crowning is recommended for operative vaginal birth in Australia/UK practice to reduce obstetric anal sphincter injury (OASIS). Midline episiotomy is associated with markedly higher OASIS risk and should not be used (RANZCOG OVB; RCOG).

new_options.text: Cut the midline episiotomy as the registrar suggests to maximise outlet space
new_options.rationale: Midline episiotomy increases the risk of third- and fourth-degree perineal tears, especially with forceps; this is an anti-pattern in Australian practice (RANZCOG OVB).

new_options.text: Cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 30 degrees from the midline before crowning
new_options.rationale: The recommended angle is 60 degrees from the midline at crowning, not 30 degrees and not before crowning; a shallow angle approaches a midline cut and increases OASIS risk (RANZCOG OVB).

new_options.text: Avoid episiotomy entirely as routine episiotomy is not recommended
new_options.rationale: Routine episiotomy is not recommended for spontaneous birth, but selective mediolateral episiotomy is recommended for forceps delivery to reduce OASIS (RANZCOG OVB).

new_options.text: Cut a J-shaped episiotomy starting midline then curving laterally at crowning
new_options.rationale: J-shaped episiotomy is not standard practice and risks the same OASIS extension as a midline cut before the curve (RANZCOG OVB).

verdicts.new_stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 39+5 has just had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3500 g infant. The baby is on the maternal chest, Apgar 9 at 1 minute, with delayed cord clamping at 90 seconds. Booked BP at 36 weeks was 138/86 and her booking BP today 1 hour ago was 145/92; she has been on labetalol for gestational hypertension since 32 weeks. She has no proteinuria and was not on magnesium sulfate. Maternal observations are now BP 150/95, HR 90, EBL so far 200 mL, uterus palpable at the umbilicus. Active management of the third stage is planned per unit protocol.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which uterotonic is the most appropriate component of active management for this woman?

new_options.text: Oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder, controlled cord traction and uterine massage
new_options.rationale: Oxytocin 10 IU IM is the first-line uterotonic for active management of the third stage in Australian practice and is safe in hypertensive women. Ergometrine and Syntometrine are contraindicated in hypertension (RANZCOG; WHO).

new_options.text: Syntometrine (ergometrine 500 microg + oxytocin 5 IU) IM with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
new_options.rationale: Syntometrine contains ergometrine, which is contraindicated in hypertension (gestational, chronic, or pre-eclampsia) because of severe vasoconstriction and stroke risk (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Ergometrine 500 microg IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder and uterine massage
new_options.rationale: Ergometrine alone is contraindicated in hypertension; this is a classic anti-pattern in obstetric pharmacology (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Misoprostol 600 microg orally with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
new_options.rationale: Misoprostol is second-line where oxytocin is unavailable (e.g. home birth, resource-limited settings); oxytocin remains first-line in Australian hospital settings (RANZCOG; WHO).

new_options.text: Carbetocin 100 microg IV bolus with immediate cord clamping and uterine massage
new_options.rationale: Carbetocin is used selectively for elective caesarean section in Australia, not routine vaginal birth; routine carbetocin is not the standard active-management agent here (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 27-year-old woman with focal epilepsy attends her GP for preconception counselling. She has been seizure-free for 4 years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily as monotherapy, prescribed by her neurologist who has been unable to wean her without seizure recurrence. She is in a stable relationship, drinks no alcohol, BMI 24, no other medications. There is no personal or family history of neural tube defects. She wishes to conceive within the next 6 months and has not yet started any vitamins. She has read online that 'folic acid 5 mg' is recommended in epilepsy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate preconception advice?

new_options.text: Continue valproate, start folic acid 5 mg daily and aim to conceive in 6 months
new_options.rationale: Valproate is highly teratogenic (NTD, cardiac, craniofacial, cognitive impairment) and is unsafe in any woman of reproductive age unless a Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) is in place and no alternatives exist. Folate alone does not mitigate the teratogenic risk (RANZCOG; TGA valproate PPP; AMH).

new_options.text: Refer to neurology for transition to a less teratogenic antiepileptic (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) and start folic acid 5 mg daily, deferring conception until stable on the new agent
new_options.rationale: Valproate must be avoided in pregnancy where possible. Neurology should switch to lamotrigine or levetiracetam, conception is deferred until seizure-free on the new agent, and folic acid 5 mg daily is started preconception until 12 weeks (RANZCOG; AMH; TGA valproate PPP).

new_options.text: Stop valproate today without neurology input and start folic acid 400 mcg daily
new_options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of antiepileptics risks status epilepticus, which carries higher maternal and foetal mortality than the drug itself. Transition must be supervised (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Continue valproate and add lamotrigine to allow pregnancy on combination therapy
new_options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk; valproate must be removed entirely if possible (RANZCOG; AMH).

new_options.text: Continue valproate and start folic acid 400 mcg daily, the standard dose for low-risk women
new_options.rationale: Folic acid 5 mg, not 400 mcg, is the recommended preconception dose for women on antiepileptics; even then, valproate must be replaced (RANZCOG; AMH).

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 9 weeks gestation has had asthma since childhood, well-controlled on budesonide 200 mcg bd plus salbutamol PRN. Over the past month she has needed reliever 4-5 times per week with two episodes of nocturnal cough. Spirometry today: FEV1 78% predicted (baseline 95% pre-pregnancy). No infective symptoms, no exposure changes. She tells you a friend advised her to stop the budesonide because 'steroids cross the placenta'. She is up to date with influenza vaccination and folic acid.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her asthma now?

new_options.text: Continue current budesonide 200 mcg bd and reassess in 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Her control has deteriorated (reliever >2/week, nocturnal symptoms, FEV1 drop). Continuing the same dose accepts uncontrolled asthma, which carries higher risk to mother and foetus than dose escalation (RANZCOG; TSANZ).

new_options.text: Step up inhaled corticosteroid (e.g. budesonide-formoterol combination) per the Australian Asthma Handbook and continue throughout pregnancy
new_options.rationale: Uncontrolled asthma is more dangerous to the pregnancy than inhaled corticosteroids. The Australian Asthma Handbook recommends standard step-up of inhaled therapy in pregnancy, including ICS-LABA combinations. Stopping ICS is the anti-pattern to avoid (RANZCOG; TSANZ).

new_options.text: Stop budesonide and use salbutamol alone for the duration of pregnancy
new_options.rationale: Stopping ICS in pregnancy is associated with worse asthma control and adverse pregnancy outcomes including pre-eclampsia, prematurity and low birth weight (TSANZ).

new_options.text: Switch to oral prednisolone 10 mg daily for the duration of pregnancy
new_options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are reserved for exacerbations, not maintenance, due to maternal side effects; inhaled escalation is preferred (TSANZ).

new_options.text: Switch to montelukast 10 mg daily as monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Montelukast may be continued if it was being used pre-pregnancy but is not first-line monotherapy; ICS-based step-up is preferred (TSANZ).

verdicts.new_stem: A 26-year-old nulliparous woman presents to the ED with 2 days of right iliac fossa pain and scant dark vaginal spotting at LMP+7 weeks. Previous PID 3 years ago, treated. No contraception. On arrival: HR 132, BP 88/52, RR 22, SpO2 98%, peripherally cool with delayed capillary refill 4 seconds, pale, conversing but anxious. Abdominal guarding, rebound tenderness across the right iliac fossa, distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Cervical excitation, right adnexal tenderness with palpable fullness. Urine beta-hCG positive. Bedside FAST shows free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison's pouch. IV access x2 has been obtained; group and hold and crossmatch sent.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Transfer immediately to operating theatre for diagnostic laparoscopy with potential laparotomy and activate the massive transfusion protocol
new_options.rationale: Haemodynamic instability (shock), positive FAST and clinical features of ruptured ectopic mandate immediate operative management without delay for further imaging. Theatre transfer with MTP activation is the correct multi-step decision (RANZCOG ectopic).

new_options.text: Perform an urgent transvaginal ultrasound to confirm location of pregnancy before further action
new_options.rationale: Imaging confirmation is appropriate for haemodynamically stable women, but a shocked patient with positive FAST should not be delayed for TVUS; immediate theatre is required (RANZCOG ectopic).

new_options.text: Administer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 and admit for monitoring
new_options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated in suspected rupture and haemodynamic instability; medical management requires stable patient and low/declining beta-hCG (RANZCOG ectopic).

new_options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease and reassess in 6 hours
new_options.rationale: Although she has prior PID, the clinical picture is shock with positive pregnancy test and free fluid; antibiotic-only treatment misses ruptured ectopic and is fatal (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours and discharge home with safety-net advice
new_options.rationale: Discharging a shocked patient with positive pregnancy and free fluid is unsafe and likely fatal (RANZCOG ectopic).

verdicts.discard_reason: HPV 16 + HSIL -> direct colposcopy is straightforward pattern matching; a more challenging NCSP scenario already exists at obgyn-006 (HPV non-16/18 + pHSIL) and at obgyn-gynonc-003. Adding a third pure pathway question would be redundant.

verdicts.new_stem: A 23-year-old woman G2P0 presents to ED with 2 days of increasing vaginal bleeding and crampy abdominal pain at 13+0 weeks gestation by ultrasound 1 week ago that showed a live intrauterine pregnancy. She is haemodynamically stable, cervix closed on speculum, uterus appropriate for dates. She is blood group O Rh(D) negative, antibody screen negative, partner Rh positive. She has not yet received any anti-D in this pregnancy. She previously had a TOP at 10 weeks last year and was given anti-D 250 IU IM at that time.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate anti-D management now?

new_options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
new_options.rationale: From 13 weeks onwards, the recommended sensitising-event dose is 625 IU IM in Australia (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Administer anti-D 625 IU IM within 72 hours now
new_options.rationale: At 13 weeks with a sensitising event (significant vaginal bleeding), Australian NBA guidance recommends 625 IU IM anti-D within 72 hours in non-sensitised RhD-negative women (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Administer routine antenatal anti-D 1500 IU IM now to cover this event and the 28-week dose
new_options.rationale: The 1500 IU dose is for routine prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks, not for sensitising events at 13 weeks; the bleeding-event dose is 625 IU (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Withhold anti-D as the cervix is closed and bleeding is settling
new_options.rationale: Sensitising events after 12 weeks require anti-D regardless of whether bleeding has settled or the cervix is closed (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Withhold anti-D as she received it last year and the half-life provides ongoing protection
new_options.rationale: Anti-D protection lasts approximately 12 weeks; protection from last year is gone and a fresh dose is required (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour has SROM during VE (cervix 6 cm) and the midwife palpates a pulsatile cord at the introitus. CTG shows sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm despite left lateral position. The on-call obstetric registrar and anaesthetist are 5 minutes away, theatre is being prepared and an emergency category-1 caesarean section has been called. The woman is in a single delivery room (no nearby toilets, no easy access to Foley-bag elevation kit). Maternal vital signs are stable.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate immediate action while awaiting theatre?

new_options.text: Place the woman in knee-chest position, elevate the presenting part manually with a vaginal hand, and instil 500-700 mL normal saline into the bladder via Foley catheter to maintain elevation; administer tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC)
new_options.rationale: Cord prolapse management is: lift presenting part off cord (manual elevation +/- bladder filling, knee-chest or Trendelenburg), tocolysis if contractions, minimise cord handling, immediate category-1 caesarean. Bladder filling allows safe transfer to theatre (RANZCOG cord prolapse).

new_options.text: Instruct active maternal pushing to expedite vaginal delivery before theatre arrives
new_options.rationale: Active pushing is inappropriate at 6 cm with foetal bradycardia and prolapsed cord; immediate caesarean is required (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Manually replace the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
new_options.rationale: Manual replacement is not recommended due to umbilical vessel spasm risk; the cord is left in situ with the presenting part elevated (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Repeat digital VE every 5 minutes to assess for cervical progress before theatre
new_options.rationale: Repeated examinations stimulate the cord, worsening spasm; the focus is on presenting part elevation and theatre transfer (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Wrap the cord in warm saline-soaked gauze outside the vagina and await spontaneous resolution
new_options.rationale: Although warm saline gauze may be applied if the cord is exposed, the priority is presenting-part elevation and immediate operative delivery, not awaiting resolution (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 33+5 weeks presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound at 32 weeks showed an anterior placenta completely covering the internal os (major praevia). She is haemodynamically stable, bleeding has settled, CTG reassuring, cervical exam deferred. She has had no previous corticosteroid courses this pregnancy. The plan is admission for monitoring and elective caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. She is keen on whatever course will best protect her baby's lung function.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate corticosteroid plan?

new_options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, now
new_options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for women at risk of preterm birth before 34+6 weeks. At 33+5 with active bleeding from major praevia and likely early delivery, a single course is indicated (RANZCOG; RCOG). 'Late preterm' (34+0 to 36+6) ACS is selective rather than universal.

new_options.text: Administer no corticosteroids as she is now beyond 34 weeks
new_options.rationale: She is 33+5, which is below 34+0, and the indication is firmly within the standard window (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM
new_options.rationale: The complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; a single dose has reduced benefit (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer weekly betamethasone 11.4 mg IM until delivery
new_options.rationale: Routine repeated weekly courses are not recommended; a single rescue course may be considered if delivery is anticipated within 7 days and >=7 days have passed since the first (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM, four doses 12-hourly, in place of betamethasone, on the basis of superior neurodevelopmental outcomes
new_options.rationale: Dexamethasone is an equivalent alternative regimen but Australian practice predominantly uses betamethasone; the question is about whether to give ACS, not which regimen is superior (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 25-year-old primipara who had a spontaneous vaginal delivery 5 days ago presents to the postnatal clinic with fever, lower abdominal pain and heavy malodorous lochia. She is breastfeeding without difficulty. Examination: T 38.7, HR 108, BP 110/68, RR 18; the uterus is bulky and tender, lochia heavy and offensive; breasts full but no focal tenderness or erythema; perineal repair healing; calves soft. Bedside urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG negative. WCC 16.2, CRP 110, Hb 102. She had a 4th-degree tear at delivery, repaired in theatre, with a single-shot prophylactic dose of cefazolin and metronidazole. She has no allergies.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily as outpatient
new_options.rationale: She has signs of sepsis (HR 108, T 38.7, WCC 16.2, CRP 110) and warrants admission and IV antibiotics, not oral outpatient therapy (eTG; RANZCOG postpartum infection).

new_options.text: Admit and commence IV ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole until clinical improvement, then de-escalate to oral
new_options.rationale: Postpartum endometritis with sepsis features requires admission and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Gram-positives, Gram-negatives and anaerobes. Therapeutic Guidelines recommend ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, with de-escalation once afebrile for 24-48 hours (eTG; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient
new_options.rationale: Doxycycline is for PID outpatient regimen; it is inadequate for postpartum endometritis with sepsis, and is contraindicated in breastfeeding (eTG).

new_options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy until afebrile, then discharge
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy misses anaerobic cover; combination therapy with metronidazole or use of triple regimen is needed (eTG).

new_options.text: Withhold antibiotics and arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound to exclude retained products before treatment
new_options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics should not be delayed in sepsis; imaging may follow but treatment is started within the first hour of recognition (Sepsis Six / eTG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Topic substantially duplicates obgyn-batch-022 (placenta praevia at late preterm with corticosteroid decision). Two questions on the same exact scenario do not add bank value.

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 22+5 weeks presents with painful regular contractions and cervical dilation 3 cm with bulging membranes. Foetal ultrasound: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal, estimated foetal weight 540 g (50th centile). She has been counselled with the neonatology team. After detailed discussion of likely outcomes at periviable gestation, the family has indicated they wish for active resuscitation if the infant is liveborn.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding antenatal corticosteroids?

new_options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, since active resuscitation is planned
new_options.rationale: RANZCOG and Australian/New Zealand neonatal consensus now support antenatal corticosteroids from 22+0 weeks when active neonatal resuscitation is planned, as ACS reduces neonatal mortality and major morbidity. Below 22 weeks ACS is not recommended (RANZCOG ACS; ANZNN periviable consensus).

new_options.text: Withhold ACS as gestation is below 23 weeks where benefit is uncertain
new_options.rationale: Updated guidance supports ACS from 22+0 weeks if active resuscitation is intended, reflecting recent evidence of reduced mortality (RANZCOG; ANZNN).

new_options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM only
new_options.rationale: A complete course is two doses; partial courses have reduced effect though some benefit if delivery is imminent (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 6 hours for four doses
new_options.rationale: The standard dexamethasone regimen is 6 mg IM 12-hourly for four doses, not 6-hourly (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Defer the decision until membranes rupture and only give if delivery is within 24 hours
new_options.rationale: ACS is given to all women at risk of preterm birth within the next 7 days; deferring until membrane rupture loses the window of benefit (RANZCOG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Duplicates PCOS diagnosis content covered by revised obgyn-016 and obgyn-repro-002 (already discarded). The bank already has stronger PCOS questions.

verdicts.new_stem: A 16-year-old girl reports 8 months of severe crampy lower abdominal pain with each period, missing school for 1-2 days each cycle. Her cycles are regular at 28 days with menarche at 13. She has tried regular paracetamol 1 g qid with minimal benefit. There is no pain outside of menses, no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no abnormal bleeding, no urinary or bowel symptoms and no fever. Examination is normal. Pregnancy test negative. She is otherwise well, has no contraindication to NSAIDs and no medication allergies. Her mother is concerned and asks whether laparoscopy is required to exclude endometriosis before further treatment.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Commence regular naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 3-5 days starting 1-2 days before menses, with review at 3 months
new_options.rationale: Primary dysmenorrhoea is diagnosed clinically; first-line is regular NSAIDs taken pre-emptively, not laparoscopy. NSAID failure after a 3-month trial, plus consideration of COCP, would precede invasive workup (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy now to exclude endometriosis
new_options.rationale: Laparoscopy is invasive and is reserved for refractory pain after NSAID +/- COCP trial or features suggestive of secondary dysmenorrhoea; it is not first-line in a teenager with classic primary dysmenorrhoea (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

new_options.text: Continue paracetamol 1 g four times daily and add codeine 30 mg qid as needed
new_options.rationale: Opioids are not first-line in adolescent dysmenorrhoea and risk dependence; NSAIDs have stronger evidence (eTG).

new_options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (goserelin) to induce amenorrhoea
new_options.rationale: GnRH agonists are not used in adolescents without confirmed endometriosis due to bone density effects; NSAIDs are first-line (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Reassure and recommend transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation as first-line
new_options.rationale: TENS is a useful adjunct but is not first-line pharmacological therapy; NSAIDs remain first-line (RANZCOG; Cochrane).

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with known fundal fibroid (8 cm at 20-week scan) presents with 36 hours of severe localised pain over the uterine fundus, mild nausea, and a low-grade temperature of 37.6. There is no vaginal bleeding, no leaking of fluid, no contractions and no urinary symptoms. Examination: tender over the fundal fibroid, no peritonism, normal bowel sounds, uterus otherwise soft. CTG reactive, foetus active. Ultrasound: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and central cystic change, no free fluid, normal placental position, normal Dopplers. White cell count mildly elevated 14, CRP 35, urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG appropriate for gestation.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

new_options.text: Admit for analgesia (paracetamol +/- short-course oral or IV opioids), hydration, monitoring of foetal wellbeing and serial CTG, with avoidance of NSAIDs at this gestation
new_options.rationale: Red degeneration of a fibroid in pregnancy is treated conservatively: analgesia, rest, hydration and foetal monitoring. NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, indomethacin) are avoided after 30 weeks due to ductal closure risk but more importantly are best avoided across the third trimester; opioids are acceptable short-term. Surgery and tocolysis are not indicated unless complications develop (RANZCOG fibroids; eTG).

new_options.text: Commence indomethacin 50 mg orally tds for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Indomethacin has anti-inflammatory effect on degenerating fibroids but risks premature ductal constriction and oligohydramnios in the third trimester; safer analgesia is preferred (eTG; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Proceed to elective myomectomy at this gestation
new_options.rationale: Antenatal myomectomy is high-risk (haemorrhage, foetal loss) and is reserved for refractory or torted pedunculated lesions; not appropriate for typical red degeneration (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Diagnose placental abruption and proceed to immediate caesarean section
new_options.rationale: Abruption presents with bleeding, woody uterus and foetal distress; her presentation is localised fibroid pain with reassuring CTG. Misdiagnosis exposes mother and foetus to unnecessary surgery (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed septic fibroid
new_options.rationale: Mild WCC/CRP rise is expected in red degeneration; without features of sepsis or chorioamnionitis, antibiotics are not indicated (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 14+0 weeks attends a recurrent-pregnancy-loss clinic. She has had two previous second-trimester losses at 20 and 22 weeks, both with painless cervical dilation and rapid delivery of a liveborn infant who died shortly after delivery. No infection, no bleeding, no abruption identified at either loss. There are no other risk factors for preterm birth (no smoking, no LLETZ, no Mullerian anomaly, no DES exposure). Current TVUS cervical length 38 mm with closed internal os. She is otherwise well and asks specifically about the option of a prophylactic suture.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

new_options.text: Offer a history-indicated cervical cerclage at 12-14 weeks now
new_options.rationale: Two or more previous second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency are the classical indication for history-indicated (prophylactic) cerclage placed at 12-14 weeks. Serial surveillance alone is less effective for this pattern (RANZCOG cerclage; RCOG Green-top 60).

new_options.text: Serial TVUS cervical length every 2 weeks, with ultrasound-indicated cerclage only if length falls below 25 mm before 24 weeks
new_options.rationale: Surveillance with ultrasound-indicated cerclage is preferred when there is only one prior loss; two or more typical insufficiency losses warrant history-indicated cerclage (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks without cerclage
new_options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is for prevention of preterm birth where the cervix is short on USS; it does not replace history-indicated cerclage in classical cervical insufficiency (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Bed rest at home from 16 weeks with avoidance of intercourse and exercise
new_options.rationale: Bed rest has no evidence of benefit and may increase VTE risk; it should not replace cerclage (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Await spontaneous cervical change and place emergency rescue cerclage only if dilation occurs
new_options.rationale: Rescue cerclage carries higher risks and lower success; in classical insufficiency, planned history-indicated cerclage is superior (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 weeks presents with reduced foetal movements for 12 hours. Bedside ultrasound confirms intrauterine foetal demise. She is haemodynamically stable, no bleeding, no contractions. The senior registrar has confirmed and broken the news, with bereavement midwifery support called. She is keen to take the baby home and is asking whether she could await spontaneous labour rather than be induced now.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

new_options.text: Counsel that delaying delivery beyond approximately 48 hours after foetal death increases the risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation, and offer induction of labour as the preferred option, while supporting her preferences
new_options.rationale: Prolonged retention of a dead foetus risks DIC (developing significantly after 4 weeks but rising from 2 days onward). Induction of labour is generally recommended; expectant management beyond a short window requires close haematological monitoring (RANZCOG stillbirth).

new_options.text: Proceed to category 1 caesarean section as soon as theatre is available
new_options.rationale: Caesarean is reserved for maternal indications (e.g. praevia, abruption with maternal compromise); vaginal birth after IUFD is the default and avoids surgical risk (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Discharge home and await spontaneous labour at term
new_options.rationale: Awaiting spontaneous labour to 40 weeks risks DIC and is not standard practice; induction is preferred (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer betamethasone now and induce labour in 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role after foetal demise; delaying for that purpose increases DIC risk (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Repeat ultrasound and CTG in 24 hours to reconfirm the diagnosis before discussing options
new_options.rationale: Diagnosis has already been confirmed by ultrasound; deferring the conversation prolongs distress and DIC risk (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 at 38+0 weeks presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and minimal vaginal bleeding. Examination: tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, fundal height larger than expected. HR 128, BP 88/54, RR 24, SpO2 97%, peripherally cool. CTG: foetal bradycardia 70 bpm with absent variability. She has chronic hypertension on labetalol and was a smoker until early pregnancy. Bedside ultrasound: posterior placenta, no praevia, retroplacental haematoma visible. Bloods sent: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 92, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section
new_options.rationale: This is placental abruption with maternal shock, foetal bradycardia and early DIC. Immediate category 1 caesarean section with MTP activation is required; both mother and foetus are in extremis (RANZCOG abruption; ANZCOR).

new_options.text: Commence tocolysis with nifedipine and observe with serial CTG
new_options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in abruption with maternal compromise and foetal distress; immediate delivery is needed (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Insert two large-bore IV cannulae, give 2 L crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
new_options.rationale: Resuscitation occurs concurrently with delivery preparation, not as an isolated step; delaying delivery worsens outcomes for both (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Attempt artificial rupture of membranes to expedite vaginal delivery
new_options.rationale: Vaginal birth may be considered for abruption only when mother and foetus are stable and labour is advanced; with shock and bradycardia, caesarean is required (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Transfer to tertiary centre for higher-level obstetric input
new_options.rationale: She cannot be transferred safely; on-site immediate delivery is required (RANZCOG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Substantively duplicates obgyn-batch-022 (major praevia at late preterm) and obgyn-batch-030 (already discarded). One praevia question is sufficient in the bank.

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramps. Speculum: cervical os is open with a small amount of tissue at the os. TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 8 mm, no retained products clearly visible, no adnexal mass, no free fluid. Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today (was 8500 IU/L two days ago). She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 122, blood group A Rh-positive, no allergies. She has had no prior surgery and is keen to be home with her partner. The team is choosing between expectant, medical and surgical management.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Offer expectant, medical (misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) management options, and support her choice
new_options.rationale: This is an incomplete or completing miscarriage in a haemodynamically stable woman. Australian guidance (RANZCOG, eTG) supports shared decision-making between expectant, medical and surgical management with comparable safety; choice depends on patient preference, bleeding and availability.

new_options.text: Proceed directly to suction curettage in theatre today
new_options.rationale: Surgical management is one option but not mandatory; expectant and medical are equally valid in a stable patient (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer single-dose methotrexate as the pregnancy may be ectopic
new_options.rationale: TVUS confirms intrauterine pregnancy products and beta-hCG is falling appropriately; ectopic management is not indicated (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion to expedite expulsion of retained products
new_options.rationale: Oxytocin is not standard for first-trimester miscarriage; misoprostol is the medical option (eTG).

new_options.text: Discharge home with no follow-up; the falling beta-hCG indicates resolution
new_options.rationale: Follow-up beta-hCG to confirm trend to negative and a follow-up review are required to exclude retained products or molar pregnancy (RANZCOG).
